states.question: What is the recurrence step for LCS of strings s1 and s2 if characters match (`s1[i] == s2[j]`)?
choices: B: lcs(i, j) = max(lcs(i-1, j), lcs(i, j-1))
states.explanation: If characters match, they belong to the common subsequence; we add 1 and solve for the remaining substrings.

states.analogy_description: Biologists match DNA strings. Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) helps find matching gene sequences that occur in the same order but not necessarily consecutively.

whiteboard_data.title: LCS DP Table Fill
whiteboard_data.description: Tracing subproblem matches across matrix coordinates.

steps.details: Chars match. Set cell value to 1 + diagonal cell value.
Mismatch. Set cell to max of left and top cell values.

states.exercise_description: Write a recursive LCS function `lcs_rec(s1, s2, i, j)` that returns the length of the longest common subsequence.
Write a bottom-up tabulated LCS function `lcs_tab(s1, s2)` that returns the LCS length.

states.prompt: Explain Applications of Dynamic Programming in your own words to a peer. Use an analogy from daily life.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Substring and Subsequence?
flashcards.back: Substrings must occur consecutively in the string; Subsequences do not need to be consecutive.

flashcards.front: What is the runtime of bottom-up LCS?
flashcards.back: O(M * N) where M and N are the string lengths.
